thread_tweets.text:
the demand comes from anthropic and openai having infinite access to capital, which they use to subsidize (run training and inference at a loss)

once they can no longer do that for whatever reason, demand necessarily shrinks substantially. they’re pouring like $100B into the market every quarter. like you said this can’t last forever

they’re essentially front-loading all the demand now instead of letting it show up later when the models are more capable, to increase their chances of winning the race. that’s the essence of the vc capital model

so the demand you’re seeing now is already dislocated. once the models become materially more capable, demand probably goes down, because the race will be over
thread_tweets.full_text:

thread_tweets.text:
i’m not disagreeing with you. what i’m saying is right now openai and anthropic are front-running future gpu demand. and they’re insensitive to price because they currently have infinite access to capital and they want to get to the finish line first

as we both seem to agree, this regime will eventually end. their access to capital will shrink and their willingness to spend all of it at any price will be over. once that happens gpu prices go down

of course as prices go down demand will show up from other businesses and consumers. but it’ll not be enough to fully replace the current demand from anthropic and openai because they’re already compressing years of future gpu demand into each quarter

tweets.text:
One of Ethereum's biggest upgrades is now in the final testing phase.

Mainnet is loosely targeted for the second half of 2026, and one of the EF devs is already calling it the biggest fork since the Merge.

These are some of the planned upgrades:

→ ePBS (EIP-7732): block-building gets baked into Ethereum itself
→ Block-level access lists (EIP-7928): blocks start running in parallel
→ Gas repricing: compute gets cheaper, state growth gets pricier
→ Gas limit: increased over time from ~60M toward 200M

IMO, the most significant change is ePBS.

Right now, close to 90% of blocks are built by outside builders and passed through offchain relays like MEV-Boost, which is critical infrastructure that Ethereum doesn't control.

ePBS builds that step directly into Ethereum, which cuts out the relay middleman and the ordering/censorship risk that comes with it.

Do you think this will put ETH into the spotlight again?

tweets.text:
Deal or no deal? Once again, that's the market's question.

Here are my Sunday thoughts on #uranium, #gold, #silver, #copper, #fluorspar and #potash.

A week ago, investors were debating whether a US-Iran agreement would stabilize the region and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Today, the picture is far less clear. Iran has once again announced the closure of Hormuz, reminding markets that geopolitics can overwhelm diplomacy overnight.

I still believe some form of agreement is the most likely outcome. Iran needs oil revenues. The US wants lower energy prices, lower inflation, and eventually lower interest rates. The incentives remain powerful. But the road to a lasting deal may be far more complicated than many expected.

What continues to impress me most is uranium. Despite one of the most significant geopolitical disruptions in years, the long-term uranium price continues to trend higher. Utilities still face the same challenge: growing reactor demand and limited future mine supply.

Gold and silver have pulled back, but if tensions ease and oil prices move lower, I expect markets to begin pricing in future rate cuts again. A weaker USD, improving liquidity, and rising sovereign debt levels would be supportive for both metals.

Copper remains one of my highest-conviction long-term themes. AI infrastructure, data centers, electrification, grid expansion, and EV adoption all require enormous amounts of copper, while major discoveries remain scarce and permitting timelines continue to lengthen.

One mineral that deserves far more attention is fluorspar. It is essential for uranium conversion, steelmaking, aluminum production, semiconductors, batteries, and numerous chemical processes. As governments push to secure domestic critical mineral supply chains, fluorspar projects in stable jurisdictions could become increasingly strategic.

And it's not just energy security that matters. It's food security too. Disruptions in energy markets often ripple through fertilizer markets. Higher energy and transportation costs ultimately affect agricultural production. That's one reason I remain bullish on potash.

The world needs more energy. More electricity. More infrastructure. More critical minerals. And more food.

That's why I remain extremely bullish on uranium, copper, gold, silver, fluorspar, and potash over the coming years.

Uranium > $300, gold > $10,000, silver > $200, and substantially higher copper prices would not surprise me at all.

The long-term story remains intact.

Just my thoughts. Please do your own DD.

Follow me for regular market updates and visit my profile to see my top picks.

A week ago, investors were debating whether a US-Iran agreement would stabilize the region and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Today, the picture is far less clear. Iran has once again announced the closure of Hormuz, reminding markets that geopolitics can overwhelm diplomacy overnight.

I still believe some form of agreement is the most likely outcome. Iran needs oil revenues. The US wants lower energy prices, lower inflation, and eventually lower interest rates. The incentives remain powerful. But the road to a lasting deal may be far more complicated than many expected.

What continues to impress me most is uranium. Despite one of the most significant geopolitical disruptions in years, the long-term uranium price continues to trend higher. Utilities still face the same challenge: growing reactor demand and limited future mine supply.

Gold and silver have pulled back, but if tensions ease and oil prices move lower, I expect markets to begin pricing in future rate cuts again. A weaker USD, improving liquidity, and rising sovereign debt levels would be supportive for both metals.

Copper remains one of my highest-conviction long-term themes. AI infrastructure, data centers, electrification, grid expansion, and EV adoption all require enormous amounts of copper, while major discoveries remain scarce and permitting timelines continue to lengthen.

One mineral that deserves far more attention is fluorspar. It is essential for uranium conversion, steelmaking, aluminum production, semiconductors, batteries, and numerous chemical processes. As governments push to secure domestic critical mineral supply chains, fluorspar projects in stable jurisdictions could become increasingly strategic.

And it's not just energy security that matters. It's food security too. Disruptions in energy markets often ripple through fertilizer markets. Higher energy and transportation costs ultimately affect agricultural production. That's one reason I remain bullish on potash.

The world needs more energy. More electricity. More infrastructure. More critical minerals. And more food.

That's why I remain extremely bullish on uranium, copper, gold, silver, fluorspar, and potash over the coming years.

Uranium > $300, gold > $10,000, silver > $200, and substantially higher copper prices would not surprise me at all.

The long-term story remains intact.

Just my thoughts. Please do your own DD.

Follow me for regular market updates and visit my profile to see my top picks.

tweets.text:
With #uranium approaching a structural supply crunch, #silver deficits persisting, #copper demand accelerating, and critical minerals becoming a strategic priority, here's where I currently see the most attractive risk/reward in the market.

My top picks across uranium, silver, copper, potash, fluorspar, and graphene 👇

These are not necessarily the safest investments. They are simply the companies where I currently see the most attractive risk/reward.

☢️ URANIUM

Producers & Developers
$CCJ – The blue-chip uranium name.
$KAP – Lowest-cost producer in the world.
$DNN – Fully permitted and advancing Wheeler River.
$NXE – One of the world's largest undeveloped uranium projects.
$UUUU – This week they received a conditional US government loan commitment of up to $725 million to expand domestic rare earth processing and strengthen critical mineral supply chains. Exposure to uranium, rare earths, and growing US strategic support.
$EU – Current U.S. production.
$UEC – Unhedged and ramping up production.
Explorers
$SYH – Drilling near Wheeler River and one of the best prospect generators in the Athabasca Basin.
$M.CN – Potentially the largest uranium deposit in the U.S. based on historic drilling and recent radiometric results. 655 Mlbs endowment in the area. Now included in URNJ, drill program about to start.
$NOBL – Recently published a maiden resource at Duck Creek with significant expansion potential.
$FUU – The team behind four discoveries. If JR and Tetra connect, this could become a monster.

🥈 SILVER
I own three established producers with great margins at current silver prices:
$HL
$AG
$PAAS
I also own these three explorers:
$HYMC – Drilling one of the largest precious metals systems in the world.
$GRSL.V – Recently reported a standout drill intercept.
$JTWO – My wildcard. Bonanza-grade silver samples suggest a much larger high-grade system.

🔌 COPPER
$CGNT – My primary copper exposure. A massive deposit with significant leverage to higher copper prices.

🌾 POTASH
$KCLI – Massive exploration target in Utah's Paradox Basin with district-scale potential.

💎 FLUORSPAR
$CTV.V – CleanTech Vanadium is moving from planning to execution. The company recently launched a major drill program at its Campbell-Crotser fluorspar project in Kentucky, targeting material for an initial underground mine plan and a planned 500 tpd processing operation.

🌐 GRAPHENE 
$HGRAF – Still my largest single holding. A unique graphene technology platform with potential applications across multiple industries.

Disclosure: I have paid partnerships with Myriad Uranium, Skyharbour Resources, J2 Metals, Noble Plains Uranium, American Critical Minerals, F3 Uranium and CleanTech Vanadium.

Not financial advice. Please do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
David Friedberg: It's not rich vs poor, it's makers vs takers.

@friedberg:

“ The great lie is that there are two sides to society, that is the rich and the poor.

And the great truth is that there are two sides that are the makers and the takers.

The lie is that the rich are unfairly rich and the poor are unfairly poor, and therefore, the poor must take from the rich.

But the truth is that it's the takers that tell you that lie, that the real truth is that artists, plumbers, electricians, woodworkers, computer scientists, people that build, people that make from all walks of life, all income levels, all wealth brackets, are the makers.

And the takers are what Sacks calls this intelligentsia, the analysts, the espousers, the armchair mechanics, the critics, the commentators, the politicians. They are the takers.

They are the people that watch the rest of society make stuff, build stuff, specifically doing things that create value for other people in society. That's what a maker is.”
tweets.full_text:

But the truth is that it's the takers that tell you that lie, that the real truth is that artists, plumbers, electricians, woodworkers, computer scientists, people that build, people that make from all walks of life, all income levels, all wealth brackets, are the makers.

And the takers are what Sacks calls this intelligentsia, the analysts, the espousers, the armchair mechanics, the critics, the commentators, the politicians. They are the takers.

They are the people that watch the rest of society make stuff, build stuff, specifically doing things that create value for other people in society. That's what a maker is.”

tweets.text:
Genuine question for those who have kids heading into overpriced colleges for the next 4 years- How do you justify that cost when AI is scaling like this?

Are universities adjusting for this? To me they feel likes bureaucratic animals where most can’t adjust to the reality fast enough.

thread_tweets.text:
Genuine question for those who have kids heading into overpriced colleges for the next 4 years- How do you justify that cost when AI is scaling like this?

Are universities adjusting for this? To me they feel likes bureaucratic animals where most can’t adjust to the reality fast enough.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Genuine question for those who have kids heading into overpriced colleges for the next 4 years- How do you justify that cost when AI is scaling like this?

Are universities adjusting for this? To me they feel likes bureaucratic animals where most can’t adjust to the reality fast enough.

thread_tweets.text: My school at the time was $40k a year. It’s now $90k a year.  The value proposition was way higher when you didn’t have AI and you could learn from an expert profession.  The cost of that expertise is dropping exponentially but the cost of the education is rising exponentially- something needs to break.

tweets.text:
I would be more worried about Mythos hacking your passwords and stealing the cash in your bank accounts moreso  than bitcoin. 

If these models are that powerful. Then anything of value digitally can be confiscated. 

What’s that old relic again?
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
NSA Director General Joshua Rudd: “Mythos broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks, but in hours.”

I’m wondering if Mythos can do this to NSA, how long before AI breaks Bitcoin bitcoin:native security wall?

https://t.co/64FfRfrjGr https://t.co/FysaPbKu5t
